甚至在大流行使我们的经济陷入困境之前，我们就面临着一个可怕的问题：我们如何为未来十年进入劳动力大军的1亿印度人找到工作？送货和移动服务初创公司不会这么做。不管那些令人讨厌的广告告诉你的是什么，学习编程成为埃隆·马斯克也不会做到这一点。我们如何创造和传播繁荣，让我们中的10亿人获得超出生存范围的生活和生计？我们的问题在规模上是独一无二的，我们不能效仿其他任何人。然而，我们可以向最好的学习。从耶稣基督出生到大约1820年，一个不幸出生为平民的人(几乎每个人都知道)知道他会度过短暂的一生，他的子孙们会度过短暂的一生，耕种地主拥有的同一块土地。我们之所以知道这一点，是因为每年的经济增长率约为0.11%，或者说一个世纪的增长率约为11%。然后，世界上有史以来最大的繁荣引擎启动了。在200年内，这个引擎将帮助我们的寿命增加近两倍，将我们从致命的疾病中拯救出来，并为我们带来电灯、润滑闪电、电视、洗衣机、拖拉机、高速公路、塑料、大规模制造、朝九晚五的工作日，当然还有互联网和可口可乐。即使其中一些改变生活的设备实际上不是在那里发明的，这个非凡的繁荣引擎也让它们大量涌现，并让它的公民能够负担得起。美利坚合众国是如何实现这一奇迹的呢？地图上的一片空白，一片广阔而咆哮的荒野，是如何在短短几个世纪内完全改变的呢？
这是这三本伟大的著作试图回答的问题(“美国人”、“财富帝国”、“美国的资本主义”)。它们有重叠之处，因为它们都将相同的历史事实编织成叙事。所有的书都是令人愉快的读物，但如果要我选一本，我会选Bhu Srinivasan的“美国人”(Americana)。这是对经济史的一次精湛、通俗易懂和有趣的重述。它的35个简短章节标志着定义时代的里程碑，从烟草、蒸汽和铁路，到计算机、金融和初创企业。但这不是对事实的线性背诵。美国生动地赋予了使这些创新及其发明者和推广者得以实现的主要社会、政治和文化力量-以及监管指导方针-的生命。这就是历史，经济史作为一个动态系统的缓慢的、经过充分检查的内部运作。这也是最伟大的创业故事，最不可能的转折点。
詹姆斯敦是美国的第一个殖民地，它的建立是由于追求利润的风险资本家支持宗教少数，采取了字面意义上的蓝海战略，并横跨北大西洋。四百年后，最终获胜的是金色拱门资本主义的宗教。该合资企业之所以成为可能，是因为两项创新，一项是技术创新，另一项是金融创新。一家股份公司允许股东共同融资并降低风险，装备齐全的船只允许进行横渡大西洋所需的不间断长途旅行。早期的投资者没有获得100倍的回报，但他们支持的风险投资最终改变了世界。
我们一开始就梦想着美国，这是一场国家悲剧的结果。印度是一个贫穷的国家，数亿人口距离饥饿只有一两步之遥，为了教育医生、工程师和科学家，印度已经投入了大量资金。然而，不知何故，这笔投资把这个国家变成了一所第三世界的精修学校，为第一世界提供人才。受过教育的人开始离开印度。他们不是在寻找自由或逃离迫害--印度过去是，现在也是世界上最大的民主国家。不，几十年来，印度人和其他数百万人一样，一直被美国资本主义的红利所吸引，而不是被其宪法的自由所吸引。我们是经济难民。
这是作者Bhu Srinivasan谈论他的父母为什么在1984年离开他们的祖国、文化、家庭和大部分自我认同，而支付移民入场费来获得经济机会。过去四十年，我们确实取得了进步，这主要得益于九十年代初的经济自由化。但我们仍有很长的路要走，要教育和留住数百万雄心勃勃的印度年轻人，否则他们可能会被迫走上同样的道路。您如何创造无与伦比的增长和繁荣引擎？
这不是一封对资本主义的厚颜无耻的情书，资本主义有其弊病和缺陷。对于社会的许多阶层来说，日益严重的不平等和机会的流失都是显而易见的，也是政治动荡的原因。然而，这不应该阻止我们欣赏其美国化身中最热门的作品。
世界上第一项发明
其他发明需要对它们以外的城镇和地区进行改造，才能真正发挥作用，这并不是一件容易的事情。发明家、规划者、有远见的政治家、官僚和金融家必须齐心协力才能实现这一目标。虽然这艘汽船极大地扩大了贸易的地理范围，但它大部分仍在沿海地区。阿巴拉契亚山脉使得整个西部地区无法进入。船显然不能通过--除非你抄近路穿过它们。你怎么能梦想承担这样的任务呢？罗伯特·富尔顿(Robert Fulton)是第一艘商业上可行的蒸汽船的开发商，他可以胜任这项工作。
1796年，当他在伦敦时，他发表了长达100多页的致密宣言，题为“运河通航改进研究”。富尔顿自封为土木工程师，接着详细介绍了多个国家的地理情况，欧洲和中国运河几个世纪的历史，以及自由流动的商业的经济优势，以及一个又一个关于能够修建大规模运河的锁定系统的技术细节。富尔顿有表格详细说明了公路运输的单位经济性与通过运河的预计成本。
仅靠这张昔日的幻灯片是不够的。这将是一项不朽的集体工程、金融和政治事业。
报纸对该计划的费用和不切实际嗤之以鼻。整个纽约的各种省级利益需要安抚、游说和联合起来。基本的问题是如何支付修建运河的费用。
愿景必须得到私营和公共部门参与者的执行和创新的支持。
在为运河融资时，纽约经纪公司向富人、海外投资者和储蓄银行出售了伊利运河债券，使其成为首批为与收入流相关的特定目的筹集资金的公共项目之一-当代市政财政术语中的“收入债券”。
最初预计的二十年内年货运量五十万吨在十年内超额完成。在开通后的20年内，伊利运河每年的运输量超过100万吨。
一夜之间，商业影响就显而易见了。来自克利夫兰的货物可能会在几天内运抵纽约。从纽约经过五大湖的水完全可以到达芝加哥。由于密西西比河已经成为通往新奥尔良的管道，美国的很大一部分地区现在可以通过水路进入。伊利运河在财务上的成功在其他州掀起了运河狂热
如果你不是美国人，你很可能没有听说过伊利运河。现在，它主要用于悠闲的巡航和娱乐的乘船。这是美国经济故事的另一个特点。随着每一次技术进步，全国范围内大规模的公共和私人伙伴关系最终将淘汰早期的基础设施。铁路取代了运河；高速公路取代了铁路；集装箱船取代了制造业中心。互联网正在取代线下零售。在每一波新的变革浪潮中，不安和无情的改变都重塑了生活，大多是向更好的方向发展。变化是艰难的，如果你是受影响的人之一，就会颠覆你的生活，这是前几千年来我们没有做好准备的事情，然而值得注意的是，每一次新的浪潮都被允许洗去旧的东西。没有其他国家像现在这样完全接受创造性破坏的想法。
这些剧变中的每一个都催生了超级富豪。运河和轮船为科尼利厄斯·范德比尔特(Cornelius Vanderbilt)的财富提供了动力，钢铁为安德鲁·卡内基(Andrew Carnegie)的繁荣提供了动力，石油为约翰·D·洛克菲勒(John D.Rockefeller)的财富提供了燃料。零售业的在线正为杰夫·贝佐斯(Jeff Bezos)带来同样的好处。他们的贡献理所当然地得到了庆祝，但如果说有什么不同的话，那就是编织在一起的故事表明，正是这个系统，在法律、政治、金融和文化力量的共同作用下，使这些巨人的出现成为可能。
指挥型经济体通常擅长大规模的基础设施建设，但不擅长使其在技术上可行的创新。当平衡偏离整体福祉时，计划经济也不允许持不同意见的声音寻求改变和新的道路。对于每个洛克菲勒，美国的体制都会产生一个艾达·塔贝尔(Ida Tarbell)。工业化肉类加工厂的过度和随之而来的残忍行为催生了厄普顿·辛克莱(Upton Sclair)这样的人。足够有趣的是，市场也奖励了这些所谓的揭黑者，他们的揭秘活动也获得了出色的销售。
还有那么多其他有趣而富有启发性的故事。以下是一些例子：在目录销售巨头西尔斯(Sears)上，亚马逊排在亚马逊之前：
1894年，西尔斯·罗巴克和安培公司(Sears，Roebuck&Amp；Co.)制作的目录大约是蒙哥马利·沃德(Montgomery Ward)的一半大小。然而，西尔斯在丰富多彩的语言方面却是蒙哥马利·沃德的两倍，尤其是在手表部分。一页上写着“汉普登手表被屠杀”。“我们说屠宰是因为我们给汉普登的报价比美国其他任何批发商都要低。”另一种描述，引导当时空中的经济民粹主义，尖叫着：“打倒垄断！打倒手表信托公司！降价了！“。
到1916年，随着T型旅行车的价格降至360美元，美国人的繁荣方程式开始受到关注：仅仅72天的劳动，不到3个月，每天5美元的工资相当于购买一辆全新的汽车。这一公式使亨利·福特成为民族英雄和国际名人，他通过每一个底盘总成和门铰链的坚固坚固直接感动了人们，他使拥有一辆汽车成为公民的一个基本标志，这是作为一个美国人意味着什么的一个基本特征。在20世纪的最初几十年里，一个经济体系能够为几乎整个国家提供汽车，这是一个令人震惊的成就，通向开放的道路象征着另一种形式的自由。
最不协调的是，主要种植香蕉的联合水果公司(United Fruit Company)也是如此。战前，该公司可能是无线电报最大的工业用户。在中美洲国家和加勒比海岛屿，该公司拥有数百万英亩的水果种植面积。该公司雇佣了这些“香蕉共和国”的大量原住民，将其土地所有权与相当于一支私人海军的土地相匹配。与其他工业品不同，水果容易腐烂。一旦一个地区的水果被采摘，比如牙买加偏远地区的香蕉，水果就需要放在有冷藏的蒸笼上。能够与海上的船只通信并将它们引导到正确的位置，消除了昂贵的腐败，并使水果采摘得到优化。在真正意义上，无线电通讯降低了纽约消费者购买香蕉的成本。
在一年内，莱维特已经建造了4000套住房，比美国任何其他开发商都多。他将这个开发项目命名为莱维顿(Levittown)。他既擅长建造房屋，也善于出售房屋。每套房子的价格都一样，7990美元，包括一台冰箱，书架和一台洗衣机。为了让莱维特镇更容易到达，莱维特提出以每月65美元的价格出租房屋。
在美国有这么多这样的金块，所以你不妨读一读。回到繁荣和就业的问题上来，它揭示的是，仅仅培育初创企业对我们没有太大帮助。我们需要在教育和基础设施、金融和结构改革以及法律和治理方面进行大规模和持续的投资，不仅是名义上的和纸面上的，而且是在执行上的。与其搭乘当前可能已经迅速消退的技术创新浪潮中的小船，我们需要一个有能力创造许多新技术的系统。人民，我们已经有了；数以亿计的人已经准备好响应号召。
note_en:
Even before the pandemic cratered our economy, we were faced with a scary question:  how do we find jobs for 100 million Indians entering the workforce over the next decade? Delivery and mobility startups aren't going to do it. Learning to code to become an Elon Musk isn't going to do it either, despite what those terribly annoying ads tell you. How do we create and spread prosperity, allowing a  billion of us access to lives and livelihoods that go beyond subsistence? Our problem is unique in scale, and we can't emulate anyone else. We can, however, learn from the best.   From the birth of Jesus Christ to about 1820, a man unfortunate enough to be born a commoner (and that was pretty much everyone) knew he would spend his short life, and his children and grandchildren would spend their short lives, tilling the same plot owned by a landlord. And we know this because economic growth was around 0.11 percent a year, or about  11 percent a century. And then, the  world's greatest ever engine of prosperity kicked into gear. Within 200 years, this engine would help us nearly triple our lifespans, save us from killer diseases, and bring us electric lighting, greased lightning, televisions, washing machines, tractors, highways, plastic, mass manufacturing, the 9-to-5 workday and of course, the internet and Coca-Cola.   Even if some of these life-changing devices weren't actually invented there, this remarkable engine of prosperity spun them out in large numbers and made them affordably available to its citizenry. How did the United States of America achieve this miraculous feat? How was an empty space on a map, a vast and roaring wilderness, so utterly transformed in a few short centuries?
   That's the question these three great books attempt to answer ( Americana,  An Empire of Wealth,  Capitalism in America). There's overlap as they are all weaving the same historical facts into narrative. And all are enjoyable reads, but if I had to pick one it would be  Americana by Bhu Srinivasan. This is a superb, accessible and entertaining retelling of economic history. Its 35 short chapters mark era-defining milestones, from tobacco, steam and railroads, to computing, finance and startups. But this is no linear recitation of facts. Americana vividly brings to life the social, political and cultural forces — and regulatory guidelines — that enabled these innovations and their inventors and popularizers.    This is history, economic history as the slowed-down, well-examined inner workings of a dynamic system.  This is also the greatest startup story with the unlikeliest of pivots.
 The founding of Jamestown, the first American colony, was made possible by profit-seeking venture capitalists backing a religious minority adopting a literal "blue ocean" strategy, and heading across the North Atlantic. Four hundred years later, it was the religion of golden-arches capitalism that ended up winning. The venture was made possible because of two innovations, one technological and the other financial. A  joint-stock company allowed shareholders to pool finances and mitigate risk, and the  full-rigged ship allowed for the long non-stop journey required to cross the Atlantic. Early investors didn't get their 100x returns, but the venture they backed ended up transforming the world.
  That we were dreaming of America in the first place was the result of a national tragedy. India, a poor country with hundreds of millions of people one or two steps removed from starvation, had gone to great expense to educate doctors, engineers, and scientists. Yet somehow this investment had turned the country into a third-world finishing school to provide the first world with talent. The educated began leaving India. They weren’t searching for freedom or fleeing persecution—India was, and is, the largest democracy in the world. No, for decades, Indians, like millions of others, had been drawn to the dividends of American capitalism, not the liberties of its constitution. We were economic refugees.
 This is the author  Bhu Srinivasan talking about why his parents left their "homeland, culture, family, and large parts of self-identity" in 1984, and paid the "immigrant's price of admission" to access economic opportunity. We have surely made progress in the last forty years, thanks mainly to the economic liberalization of the early nineties. But we still have a long way to go to to educate and then retain the millions of ambitious young Indians who might otherwise be forced to retread the same path. How do you create an unmatched engine of growth and prosperity?
 This isn't an unabashed love letter to capitalism, which has its ills and flaws. Growing inequality and the leaching of opportunity for many sections of society are all too evident and reason for political upheaval. That, however, shouldn't stop us from admiring the greatest hits of its American avatar.
   The first invention to launch America into the era of industrialization was the cotton gin. The gin (short for engine) drastically cut down the time it took to separate cotton fibers from their seeds. It would be the first of many labor-saving devices that American inventors would come up with. Freeing up time, speeding up travel, these inventions would allow for the production of surplus that not only lowered prices, but allowed America to become an important part of the global supply chain.  But what allowed these inventors to take on the risk of costly experiments, which might have taken years of frustration and tinkering? Just four years before its invention by Eli Whitney, a far more foundational invention, an abstract one, was codified in the newly drafted U.S  Constitution. T his was the first constitution in the world to protect intellectual property rights.
 The framers of the American Constitution had understood intellectual property protections as fundamental to the “progress of sciences and useful arts.” Before addressing the freedom of speech or religion through amendments, the American Constitution relegated to the federal government functions such as minting money, maintaining a postal service, and declaring and financing war, and the granting of copyrights and patents to protect “writings and discoveries.” When granted, this formal recognition of the government in effect created property rights out of thin air, based on ideas and abstractions.
 Other inventions required the remaking of towns and territories beyond them to be truly useful, and this was no easy task. Inventors, planners, visionary politicians and bureaucrats, and financiers had to work together to make this possible. While the steamboat had greatly expanded the geography of trade, it was still largely coastal. The Appalachian mountain ranges made the entire Western part inaccessible. Boats could obviously not go through -- unless you cut through them. How do you even dream of taking on such a task? Robert Fulton, the developer of the first commercially viable steamboat, was up to it.
    In 1796, while in London, he published his dense 100-plus-page manifesto titled Treatise on the Improvement of Canal Navigation. Fulton, awarding himself the title of civil engineer, went on to detail the geography of multiple nations, centuries’ worth of history on the canals of Europe and China, and the economic advantages of free-flowing commerce, along with technical detail after technical detail about a locking system that would enable large-scale canals. Fulton had tables detailing the unit economics of road-based transportation versus the projected costs via canals.
 This yesteryear slide-deck alone wouldn’t have been enough. It would have to be a monumental and collective engineering, financial, and political undertaking.
 Newspapers scoffed at the plan’s expense and impracticality. Various provincial interests throughout New York needed to be mollified, lobbied, and coalesced. The basic issue was how to pay for the canal
 Vision had to be backed up by execution and innovation from private  and public sector participants.
 In financing the canal, New York brokerage houses sold Erie Canal bonds to wealthy individuals, overseas investors, and savings banks, making it one of the first public projects to raise money for a specific purpose tied to a revenue stream—“revenue bonds” in contemporary municipal finance terms.
  The initial projection of 500,000 annual tons of goods within twenty years was exceeded within ten. Within twenty years of its opening, over a million tons per year were being transported on the Erie Canal.
 Overnight, the commercial implications were clear. Goods from Cleveland could end up in New York within days. Chicago was completely accessible by water from New York through the Great Lakes. With the Mississippi River already the conduit to New Orleans, a very large part of the United States could now be accessed by waterways. The Erie Canal’s financial success set off canal mania in other states
 If you are not from the U.S. you’ve most likely not heard of the Erie Canal. Now, it is largely used for leisurely cruises and recreational boat rides. That’s another feature of the American economic story. With every technological advance, public and private partnerships at a grand, nationwide scale would end up obsoleting earlier infrastructure. Railroads replaced canals; highways replaced railroads; container ships replaced manufacturing hubs. And the internet is replacing offline retail.  Each new wave of change, restless and relentless, remade lives, mostly for the better. Change is tough, life-upending if you are among the affected, and something the preceding millennia just did not not prepare us for, and yet it is remarkable that each new wave was allowed to wash away the old. No other country has so fully embraced the idea of creative destruction.
  Each of these upheavals also gave rise to the enormously wealthy. Canals and steamboats powered the wealth of Cornelius Vanderbilt, steel forged the prosperity of Andrew Carnegie, and oil fueled the riches of John D. Rockefeller.  The onlining of retail is doing the same for Jeff Bezos. Their contributions are rightly celebrated, but if anything, the stories woven together show  it is the system, well lubricated with legal, political, financial, and cultural forces operating together, that made the emergence of these titans possible.
 Command economies generally excel at large-scale infrastructure undertakings, but not at the innovations that make them technically possible. Command economies also do not allow for dissenting voices to seek change and new paths when the balance tips away from general well-being. For every Rockefeller, the American system produced an  Ida Tarbell. The excesses and attendant cruelties of industrialized meatpacking gave rise to the likes of  Upton Sinclair. Funnily enough, the marketplace rewarded these so-called muckrakers too with stellar sales for their exposés.
  There are so many other interesting and revelatory stories. Here are a few:  On Sears, the catalog-sales behemoth, the Amazon before Amazon:
 In 1894, Sears, Roebuck & Co. was producing a catalog about half the size of Montgomery Ward’s. Sears, however, had twice the exuberance in terms of colorful language, especially in its watch section. “Hampden Watches Slaughtered” read one page. “We say slaughtered because we are offering Hampden for less money than any other wholesale house in America.” Another description, channeling the economic populism in the air at the time, screamed: “Down with Monopoly! Down with Watch Trusts! Down with Prices!”
  As the price of the Model T Touring model dropped to $360 by 1916, the American equation for prosperity came into focus:  A mere seventy-two days of labor, less than three months, at the $5 wage per day equaled the purchase of a brand-new car. The formula made Henry Ford a national hero and an international celebrity, a man who directly touched the people through the sturdiness of every chassis assembly and door hinge, who made owning an automobile a fundamental mark of citizenship, an essential characteristic of what it meant to be an American.  That an economic system would be able to provide automobiles to virtually an entire nation in the opening decades of the twentieth century was a staggering achievement, access to the open road symbolizing another form of freedom
  Most incongruously, so did the United Fruit Company, which primarily grew bananas. Before the war, the company was perhaps the largest industrial user of wireless telegraphy. Across countries in Central America and islands in the Caribbean, the company owned millions of acres on which it grew fruit. Employing large native populations in these “banana republics,” the company matched its landholdings with the equivalent of a private navy. Unlike other industrial goods, fruit was perishable. Once an area was picked of its fruit, say, bananas in a remote part of Jamaica, the fruit needed to be placed on board a steamer with refrigeration. The ability to communicate with ships at sea and route them to the right location eliminated costly spoilage and allowed fruit picking to be optimized. In a real sense, radio communications lowered the cost of bananas for consumers in New York.
  Within a year, Levitt had built four thousand homes, more than any other developer in America. He named the development Levittown. He was as adept at selling houses as he was at building them. Pricing each home identically at $7,990, he included a refrigerator, bookshelves, and a washing machine. Making Levittown even more accessible, Levitt offered to rent the homes for $65 per month.
 There are so many such nuggets in  Americana, so you might as well just read it. Coming back to the prosperity and jobs question, what it reveals is that merely fostering startups isn’t going to do much for us. We will need large and sustained investment in education and infrastructure, financial and structural reforms, and law and governance not just in name and on paper, but in execution. And rather than hitching rides in small boats on the current wave of technical innovation, which might already be receding rapidly, we will need a system that has the capacity to create many new ones. The people, we already have; hundreds of millions ready to answer the call.
title: 创业国家：发明自己的国家
title_en: Startup Nation: The Country That Invented Itself

我当然知道，而且我目前通过Spectrum电缆到我的家庭办公室有300 Mbps的传输速度。我真正想要的是通过光纤到我家门口的千兆位。也许有一天我会得到它。但是，我确实知道的是，我不会从5G获得千兆每秒的速度。现在不行，明天不行，永远都不行。
目前，电信公司在一个接一个的广告中告诉你的很多事情都不是真的。我知道-令人震惊的消息，对吧？但是，即使按照他们的标准，5G也是相当虚假的。
让我们从名字本身开始吧。没有单一的5G。事实上，有三个不同的品种，有着非常不同的性能。
首先是T-Mobile喜欢谈论的低频段5G。那是一家提供广泛覆盖范围的保险公司。一座塔可以覆盖数百平方英里。它不是速度恶魔，但即使是20+Mbps的速度也比农村DSL坚持的3 Mbps的速度要好得多。而且，在理想情况下，它可能会给你100+Mbps的速度。(回到我的家乡西弗吉尼亚州，人口约为7200人，我会因为这样的速度而丧命。)。
然后是中频5G。它运行在1 GHz到6 GHz之间，覆盖范围约为4G的一半。你可以希望看到它的速度在200 Mbps的范围内。如果你在美国，你很少听说过这个。只有继承了Sprint的2.5 GHz中频5G的T-Mobile正在部署它。然而，它的发展速度很慢，因为它的大量潜在带宽已经被使用。
但是，大多数人想要的是延迟小于10毫秒的1Gbps速度。根据最新的NPD研究，约有40%的iPhone和33%的Android用户对获得5G非常或非常感兴趣。他们想要所有的速度，而且他们现在就想要。18%的人甚至声称他们理解5G网络频段类型之间的差异。
我不相信。因为如果他们这么做了，他们就不会这么着急买一部5G智能手机了。你看，要获得这样的速度，你必须有mmwave 5G-而且它附带了很多警告。
首先，它的射程充其量只有150米。如果你在开车，那就意味着，在5G基站无处不在之前，你的高速信号会丢失很多。实际上，在接下来的几年里，如果你在移动，你将不会看到高速5G。
而且，即使你在5G基站的覆盖范围内，任何东西-我是说任何东西-都可以阻挡它的高频信号。例如，窗户玻璃可以阻止它的死亡。因此，你可能在街角有一个5G收发器，却无法接收到良好的信号。
这有多糟糕？日本最大的移动电话服务提供商NTT DoCoMo正在研发一种新型的窗户玻璃，这样他们的mmWave 5G就可以工作了。我不知道你怎么样，但我不想花几千块钱换窗户，就为了让我的手机能用。
不过，假设你有一部5G手机，而且你确信你能获得5G服务--你真正能期待什么样的性能呢？据“华盛顿邮报”科技专栏作家杰弗里·A·福勒(Geoffrey A.Fowler)称，你可以期待看到笨拙的下蹲5G性能。听起来差不多。
而且，从技术上讲，什么是微不足道的下蹲速度？在5G手机上试试32 Mbps的AT&amp；T，在4G手机上试试34 Mbps。在T-Mobile上，我的5G手机和4G手机分别是15 Mbps和13 Mbps。&他无法查看Verizon。顺便说一下，那不是打字错误。他的4G手机比他的5G手机更快。
不只是他，因为他住在技术落后的旧金山湾区。他与几家追踪5G性能的全国性公司进行了核实。他们发现，美国三大电信网络5G并不比4G快很多。
事实上，OpenSignal报告称，美国5G用户的平均速度为33.4 Mbps。是的，比4G好，但不是&34；哇！这太棒了！&34；速度似乎是大多数人梦寐以求的。我可以补充说，这也比任何使用5G的国家都糟糕得多，除了英国。
你也只能在大约20%的时间里得到5G。这是普通的5G，其中包括T-Mobile，因为它的覆盖范围很大，而不是超高速的mmWave 5G，它的覆盖范围很小，可以实现你的梦想。除非你在毫米波收发器旁边生活或工作，否则你根本看不到承诺的速度或任何接近它们的速度。
底线是，如果你住在美国乡村的田野和树林里，你绝对应该考虑T-Mobile600 MHz 5G。但是，如果你渴望超高速，那就算了吧。我认为5G毫米波要到2022年才值这个价。
坦率地说，我最早也要到2025年才能指望它能广泛使用。而且，总有一天，我们仍然不会看到真正的千兆秒速度。
note_en:
I sure do, and I currently have 300Mbps to my home office via Spectrum cable. What I  really want is a Gigabit via fiber optic to my doorstep. Maybe I'll get it someday. But, what I do know for a fact is I'm not going to get Gigabit-per-second speeds from 5G. Not now, not tomorrow, not ever.
 At the moment, there are a lot of things the telecomms are telling you in one ad after another that's just not true. I know – shocking news right? But, even by their standards, 5G is pretty bogus.
  Let's start with the name itself.  There is no single "5G." There are, in fact, three different varieties, with very different kinds of performance.
 First, there's low-band 5G, which is the one  T-Mobile likes to talk about. That's the one offering broad coverage. A single tower can cover hundreds of square miles. It's no speed demon, but even 20+ Mbps speeds are a heck of a lot better than the 3Mbps speeds that rural DSL sticks you with. And, in ideal situations, it may give you 100+ Mbps speeds. (Back in my home county in West Virginia, population about 7,200, I'd have killed for speeds like that.)
 Then, there's mid-band 5G. This runs between 1GHz and 6GHz and it has about half the coverage of 4G. You can hope to see speeds in the 200Mbps range with it. If you're in the United States, you rarely hear about this one. Only T-Mobile, which inherited Sprint's 2.5GHz mid-band 5G, is deploying it. It's going slowly, though, because a lot of its potential bandwidth is already being used.
  But, what most people want, what most people lust for is 1Gbps speeds with less than 10 milliseconds of latency. According to a  new NPD study, about 40% of iPhone and 33% of Android users are  extremely or very interested in getting 5G. They want all that speed and they want it now. And 18% even claim they understand the difference between the 5G network band types.
  I don't believe it. Because if they did, they wouldn't be in such a rush to get a 5G smartphone. You see, to get that kind of speed you must have mmWave 5G – and it comes with a lot of caveats.
  First, it has a range, at best, of 150 meters. If you're driving, that means, until 5G base stations are everywhere, you're going to be losing your high-speed signal a lot. Practically speaking, for the next few years, if you're on the move, you're not going to be seeing high-speed 5G.
 And, even if you  are in range of a 5G base station, anything – and I mean anything – can block its high-frequency signal. Window glass, for instance, can stop it dead. So, you could have a 5G transceiver literally on your street corner and not be able to get a good signal.
 How bad is this?  NTT DoCoMo, Japan's top mobile phone service provider, is working on a new  kind of window glass, just so their mmWave 5G will work. I don't know about you, but I don't want to shell out a few grand to replace my windows just to get my phone to work.
 Let's say, though, that you've got a 5G phone and you're sure you can get 5G service – what kind of performance can you really expect? According to  Washington Post tech columnist  Geoffrey A. Fowler, you can expect to see a  "diddly squat" 5G performance. That sounds about right.
  And, technically speaking what are diddly squat speeds? Try "AT&T with 32Mbps with the 5G phone and 34Mbps on the 4G one. On T-Mobile, I got 15Mbps on the 5G phone and 13Mbps on the 4G one." He wasn't able to check Verizon. That's not a typo, by the way. His 4G phone was faster than his 5G phone.
 It wasn't just him, since he lives in that technology backwater known as the San Francisco bay area. He checked with several national firms tracking 5G performance. They found that all three major US telecom networks' 5G isn't that much faster than 4G.
 Indeed,  OpenSignal reports that  US 5G users saw an average speed of 33.4Mbps. Better than 4G, yes, but not "Wow! This is great!" speeds most people seem to be dreaming of. It's also, I might add, much worse than any other country using 5G, with the exception of the United Kingdom.
 You're also only going to get 5G about 20% of the time. That's generic 5G, which includes T-Mobile with its great coverage, not that ultrafast mmWave 5G, with its tiny coverage, of your dreams. Unless you live or work right next to an mmWave transceiver, you're simply not going to see those promised speeds or anything close to them.
 The bottom line is, if you live in the fields and woods of rural America, you should definitely consider  T-Mobile 600MHz 5G. But, if you're longing for super speed, forget about it. I don't think mmWave 5G will be worth the money until 2022.
 Frankly, I'm not counting on it being widely available until 2025 at the earliest. And, come the day it is, we'll still not see real-world Gigabit second speeds.
title: 在这一点上，5G是一个糟糕的笑话
title_en: At this point, 5G is a bad joke

note:
这个险恶的问题--至少听起来很险恶--成了尤金·伯迪克(Eugene Burdick)1964年的小说“480”的广告文案的标题。他之前的畅销书“丑陋的美国人”和“故障保险”在政界引起了轰动，新的一本承诺也会这样做。它的外套以穿孔卡片的形象为特色。该标题涉及480类选民，按地区、宗教、年龄和其他人口特征定义，如“中西部、农村、新教、低收入、女性”。许多读者对被归入其中一个盒子的想法感到畏缩。“纽约时报”的评论家称480是一部“令人震惊的小说”，并认为这是不可信的。
什么事这么令人震惊？什么是不可信的？一家公司可能利用计算机技术和行为科学来收集和处理美国公民的数据，目的是影响总统选举的想法。
在20世纪50年代和60年代，这看起来像是科幻小说。实际上，480是一个几乎不加掩饰的罗马标志，基于一家名为Simulmatics的现实生活中的公司，该公司曾秘密为约翰·F·肯尼迪(John F.Kennedy)1960年的竞选活动工作。伯迪克本人就是一名政治特工，对模拟学的创始人非常熟悉。公司的机密报告和备忘录直接写进了他的散文中。这480个类别--列在小说的附录中--是真正的Simulmatics选民类型，它的创始人之一称其为“一种曼哈顿计划在政治上的赌博”。
Simulmatics成立于1959年，历时11年。吉尔·莱波勒在她关于美国的不朽历史“这些真相”(2018年)中提到了它参与了肯尼迪的竞选活动；她已经在她的新书“如果那样的话”中讲述了这个故事。莱波勒是一位才华横溢、多产的历史学家，善于挖掘不同寻常的、具有启发性的故事。这是一部非同寻常的传奇故事，时而滑稽，时而不祥：正如她所写的，这是一段“20世纪60年代的影子历史”，因为这十年来，Simulmatics在越南战争、民权运动、伟大社会、骚乱和抗议活动的舞台上扮演着微不足道的角色。它始于发明一种新的预测行为科学的宏伟雄心，在冷战的焦虑中，研究环境越来越与日益崛起的国防机构联系在一起。它在尴尬和破产中可耻地结束了。
新保守主义未来的设计师欧文·克里斯托尔(Irving Kristol)在1964年对Simulmatics不屑一顾，认为它是“一家苦苦挣扎的小公司，尽管它在1960年为肯尼迪组织解决了几个问题，但此后一直很难维持收支平衡”，他说得没错。今天，它几乎完全被遗忘了。然而，勒波尔在其中发现了我们目前的全景电视一个看似有理的不为人知的起源故事：一个不断受到监视的世界，如果不是受到国家的监视，那么是由大公司通过预测和操纵我们的行为-包括最阴险的-包括我们作为选民的行为-来赚取巨额财富。
“Simulmatics”这个名字是“模拟”和“自动”的混合体，是科技初创公司(Teledyne，Biotronik，Microsoft)青睐的那种人造复杂的复合体，在20世纪90年代的一股逆势催生出雅虎、谷歌和Twitter等名称之前，它是一种人造复杂的复合体，受到了科技初创公司(Teledyne，Biotronik，Microsoft)的青睐。Simulmatics的创建者和总裁是前麦迪逊大道广告和公关主管爱德华·L·格林菲尔德(Edward L.Greenfield)。莱波尔形容他是一个举手投足、喝苏格兰威士忌、抽波尔购物中心(Pall Mall)的小贩。格林菲尔德将国家政治视为具有科学头脑的广告业的处女地。
这是一个相当新的想法。1952年，雄辩而博学的民主党提名人阿德莱·史蒂文森(Adlai Stevenson)反对向专业人士支付大众说服工具的费用，而德怀特·D·艾森豪威尔(Dwight D.Eisenhower)则成为第一位出现在电视广告中的总统候选人。那一年，人们嘴边挂在嘴边的一个词是“洗脑”，这是爱德华·亨特(Edward Hunter)创造的，他是狂热的反共作家，著有“红色中国的洗脑：男性思想的算计毁灭”(1951)。史蒂文森不想参与其中。共和党人在电视广告上花费了150万美元，在你了解到民主党人花费了7.7万美元之前，这似乎是一笔微不足道的钱。
当时的计算机是由数千根真空管制成的家庭大小的机器，以液汞为“存储器”。雷明顿·兰德公司为人口普查局生产的世界上最大的计算机是UNIVAC。哥伦比亚广播公司新闻在选举之夜雇佣了它-沃尔特·克朗凯特称其为“现代的奇迹，电子大脑。”它不仅仅是在数字进来的时候对它们进行统计；它的目的是在最终结果公布之前预测结果。他的同事查尔斯·科林伍德(Charles Colingwood)说：“这不是开玩笑，也不是恶作剧。”“这是一个实验，我们认为它会奏效。我们不知道，我们希望它能奏效。“。科林伍德称这台电脑为“他”：“他坐在角落里，不停地哼唱。几分钟后
计算的先驱们梦想着一种能够对大量数据进行快速计算的机器：天气预报。大气行为只是物理学，相关的方程是众所周知的。在普林斯顿高等研究院，约翰·冯·诺伊曼认为计算机很快就会使人类不仅能够预测天气，而且能够控制天气。他相信某种程度的完美，我们现在知道这是遥不可及的，因为天气是一个混乱的系统，但他发明的方法被用于在世界上所有气象中心不间断运行的超级计算机中。计算机通过模拟天气来预测天气：通过创建模型并让它们运行。
广告人埃德·格林菲尔德(Ed Greenfield)认为没有理由不对人们的行为尝试同样的方法。他在他的公司里创建了一个社会科学部门，其想法是模拟整个选民，并将投票预测卖给竞选团队。他雇佣了一个计算机专家和一位社会科学家，比尔·麦克菲和伊蒂尔·德·索拉·普尔，以及当时的政治学家尤金·伯迪克(Eugene Burdick)。他们都以这样或那样的方式痴迷于将人类行为数学化的可能性。麦克菲对编程热情洋溢：“计算机(Univac、Manniac+‘Johnniac’-我在Johnniac工作)就像是为成年男孩准备的大型电动火车。”普尔正在研究一种“社交网络”理论。他有一个想法，“任何两个美国人，任何形式的，都可以由六个或更少的熟人组成的链条联系在一起”--有人可能会说，六度分隔。
这些人是谁？“他们是世纪中叶的白人自由主义者，在那个时代，白人自由主义者不会理解不是白人或自由主义者的人，”莱波勒说。她称他们为--他们有时自称--“假设男人”(What-if Men)。经过深入研究，以优雅和激情写成的这本书生动地描绘了他们的生活，包括他们经常悲惨的妻子，他们遭受着“20世纪50年代中产阶级婚姻的糟糕交易”。在“480”中，布尔迪克将模拟人描述为“新的黑社会”：
使用计算尺、计算器和计算机的无辜和善意的人…。。这些人大多受过高等教育，其中许多人是博士，我见过的人中没有一个人对美国公众有恶意的政治图谋。然而，他们可能会从根本上重建美国的政治体系，建立一种新的政治，甚至修改受人尊敬和受人尊敬的美国制度-这些事实他们都是无辜的。
格林菲尔德想要建造一个“投票行为机器”。他还称其为“人性化机器”。它将把选民视为“在‘微观’层面上的离散单元”，它将创造一幅“当所有因素综合在一起时，整个系统是如何运作的宏观图景”。这是第一次，通过蓬勃发展的政治民调业务收集了大量数据。当格林菲尔德和他的团队在1960年大选之前组成Simulmatics时，他们购买了过去四次全国选举的盖洛普民意调查结果，对穿孔卡片进行了10万次调查。他们将问题归类为50个“问题态度”，并将这些人归入人口统计学方框。
首先，他们试图将自己的服务出售给史蒂文森，史蒂文森是他们在潜在民主党候选人中的最爱。他们提议在向实际选民披露政策立场之前，先在机器中测试这些立场。候选人应该为南方的民权大声疾呼吗？“根据我们的模型，我们将能够预测这样的演讲对人口…的1,000个子组中的每一个意味着什么。从而找出可能影响选举人投票的州，“格林菲尔德写道。这很容易让一些人觉得愤世嫉俗或狡猾。它应该会给我们留下深刻的印象，说明今天每一位技术敏锐的政治顾问都能提供什么。
当提名被肯尼迪取而代之时，他们向他的竞选团队进行了推销。它是这样的：
仿真以其快速、准确、精确、高效的特点，为政治战略家提供了独特的试管。在他采取行动之前，它会告诉他，他对某些问题的处理会对选民的投票行为产生什么影响。
这种现代智能与根据历史数据开发的模拟模型之间的关系类似于当前天气信息与气候学模型之间的关系。
肯尼迪团队委托进行了三份报告-非常紧急，因为竞争对手第一次同意进行电视辩论，而且日期越来越近。
模拟机的人工作得很快。现在没有人能说出他们的建议有多少来自哥伦比亚大学的IBM704电脑，有多少是老式的政治头脑。正如莱波勒所说，“在那些轻浮的人留下的档案踪迹中，有很多虚张声势和胡言乱语。”但这个建议似乎是好的。黑人选民在480个类别中的代表性严重不足，因为乔治·盖洛普(George Gallup)和其他民调机构避免对他们进行调查-温顺地接受吉姆·克罗(Jim Crow)压制选民的现实，并向明确不希望黑人接受民意调查的南方报纸所有者卑躬屈膝。但Simulmatics对他们进行了统计，并提出，如果不支持民权运动，民主党人就不可能获胜。他们还建议候选人坦率地谈论困扰他竞选活动的反天主教。他们敦促他将辩论视为一次机会：“尼克松面临的危险是，肯尼迪可以利用他更有风度的特点-包括热情、幽默、友谊和灵性等一系列情感。”肯尼迪做了所有模拟学建议的事情，当他在11月以微弱优势获胜时，假设男人们确信他们应该得到赞扬。
就是这样。Simulmatics对这场(或任何)政治活动的干预总和相当于几条建议，表面上是计算机生成的，但实际上至多是计算机辅助的。没有选民被操纵或欺骗。没有人的隐私受到侵犯。
肯尼迪团队认为他们购买了机密的研究报告，但模拟学人员却不顾一切地想要宣传。其中一人是托马斯·B·摩根(Thomas B.Morgan)--他是埃德·格林菲尔德(Ed Greenfield)的朋友，曾帮助哈珀编辑这三篇报道。他接近哈珀，假装是一名无私的记者，该杂志在1961年1月，也就是肯尼迪就职的那个月，刊登了他的耸人听闻的报道。“人-机器，”标题尖叫着。“这是第一份关于为民主党总统竞选秘密设计的计算设备及其对政治战略影响的报告。”这是一篇冒充揭露的吹捧文章。它引用了另一位格林菲尔德的朋友，耶鲁大学政治学家哈罗德·拉斯韦尔(Harold Lasswell)的话说，“这是社会科学的原子弹。”
今天，据说候任总统约翰·F·肯尼迪的高级顾问让一个绰号为“人-机器”的秘密设计的机器人竞选策略师开始工作，以提出影响选民的替代方法。
“纽约先驱论坛报”称肯尼迪的“秘密武器”是“一个巨大而笨重的怪物，叫做‘模拟器’。”俄勒冈州的一家报纸在社论中写道，“选民--你、我、隔壁的琼斯夫人和大学的史密斯教授”都沦落为“穿孔卡片上的小洞，或者我们的新统治者使用的任何设备”。肯尼迪人民惊慌失措，试图否认整件事。
这些仍然陌生的机器--会思考的机器--让人们感到恐惧，有好的原因，也有坏的原因。不管怎么定义，机器都不能真正思考。他们不是怪物，也不是机器人。他们可能威胁要抢走人的工作，也可能不威胁要抢走人的工作：肯尼迪本人曾在竞选活动中警告说，“日益严重的自动化危机--机器取代了人。”即便如此，对计算机的依赖似乎是对人类机构的攻击：你和我，沦落到“穿孔卡片上的小洞”。Simulmatics大肆宣扬自己的力量和威力，但也试图用例行公事的保证来消除这些担忧：“机器除了加快交流别无选择。”通过这样做，“他们恢复了在大社会中就重要问题进行现成讨论的可能性。”如今，这听起来很天真。30年后，随着互联网的兴起，人们再次听到了同样的充满希望的纯真。
当然，计算机在速度和能力上的增长速度和能力都比最乐观的假设人类所能想象的要快得多。对于Simulmatics来说，计算的希望和预测人类行为的希望捆绑在一起。“Simulmatics公司的科学家采取行动的前提是，如果他们能收集足够多人的数据，并将其输入一台机器，”莱波勒写道，“有朝一日，一切都可能是可预测的，每个人、每个人的思维都会被模拟出来，每一个行为都会被像导弹一样准确无误的有针对性的信息自动地预测，甚至是被驱动和指挥。”比尔·麦克菲(Bill McPhee)在Simulmatics上写下了他所谓的“一套思维规则”，以“再现或更好地模拟选民的思维行为”。(他是在贝尔维尤医院(Bellevue Hospital)的精神科病房里写下这些规则的，他的妻子曾在那里暂时关押过他，这是小说家可能会羞于编造的那种细节。)。
大约在同一时间，科幻小说家艾萨克·阿西莫夫(Isaac Asimov)开始了他的基础系列小说，前提是心理历史科学的想法。掌握了这门科学的先贤们可以预测帝国的兴衰：“心理历史是社会学的精髓；它是一门关于人类行为的科学，简化为数学方程。”1阿西莫夫虚构的未来不需要计算机来预测它的未来--只需要天才，用他们的公式变魔术。阿西莫夫和模拟学的不同之处在于，阿西莫夫知道他在编造一个幻想。人的行为不像台球或水分子那样可预测。大气模拟依赖于温度、压力和湿度等变量；心理科学没有发现可以简单或精确地定义为简单或精确的心理或社会量。无论我们的计算机有多强大，我们都没有科学的行为法则来养活它们。
在20世纪60年代以一场宣传风暴开始后，Simulmatics开始将其触角从竞选政治扩展到消费者营销。该公司向电影制片厂和唱片公司以及广告公司推销项目-他们认为任何人都可能相信模拟大众消费者的需求和需求的概念。莱波勒写道，它提供了“一个模拟的人口，一个微型的美国，由三千个完全有代表性但完全是虚构的人组成。”英国讽刺杂志Punch嘲笑了这种解读社会思想的新方法：“Punch建议Simulmatics的Media-Mix增加几个类别，”莱波勒写道，
包括“爱狗、平地、甜甜圈扣篮、第一牛奶”，以便能够确定，例如，周三购物、同情共和党人、“果汁早餐、抑郁症发作、轻微散光、合身地毯、粗眉毛，以及一个或多个军队表亲”的消费者是否会想要一种新的75美分粉色圆珠笔墨水。(注：这句话的意思是：“爱狗的人，平地人，甜甜圈扣篮人，第一牛奶人”，这样的人是否会想要新的75美分的粉色圆珠笔墨水，有没有共和党人的同情心，“果汁早餐，抑郁发作，轻微散光，合适的地毯，浓密的眉毛，以及军队里的一个或多个表亲。”
该公司还寻求与政府签订合同。它的社会科学家，现任麻省理工学院新政治学系主任的普尔，得到了国防部高级研究计划局(Advanced Research Project Agency)的资助，开展了一项名为ComCom项目的心理战，即共产主义通信。随着美国越来越深入地投入到越南战争中，Simulmatics也去了那里。毕竟，心理战只是政治说服军事化，这里是肥沃的新领域。“越南是我们有史以来最伟大的社会科学实验室！”普尔告诉一位同事。罗伯特·麦克纳马拉(Robert McNamara)是一位注重数字的国防部长，他希望赢得人心，并与他作为民用承包商引入的行为科学家的士兵一起。
Simulmatics及其更大的竞争对手兰德公司(RAND Corporation)在附近的西贡别墅设立了店铺。兰德的分析师之一是一位名叫丹尼尔·埃尔斯伯格(Daniel Ellsberg)的前海军陆战队队员，他后来帮助撰写并泄露了这份被称为五角大楼文件(Pentagon Papers)的毁灭性的秘密报告。这些研究人员在一场血腥的战争中是一个奇怪的存在。弗朗西斯·菲茨杰拉德(Frances Fitzgerald)在获奖历史著作“湖里的火”(Fire In The Lake)中写道：“兰德和模拟学的年轻人开着路虎(Land Rovers)在乡村四处奔走，学习‘村庄精英的向上流动’或‘土地改革与农民政治动机的相互关系’。”当她26岁到达越南时，她认为他们非常天真。
按照莱波勒的说法，模拟操作有点像小丑表演。它的工作人员几乎不会说越南语，他们把手指伸进了美国冒险的各个部分，包括臭名昭著的安抚计划以及一个名为Chieu Hoi的心理战项目。这些更多地依赖于老式的宣传方法和残酷的胁迫，而不是任何涉及特殊数据分析的东西。1966年，埃德·格林菲尔德(Ed Greenfield)带着建立一个“准军事行动研究中心”的计划来到这里。但
五十年前看起来可怕甚至不道德的事情，现在我们大多认为是理所当然的。我们不应该这样做。Facebook将其数十亿用户划分为480多个类别，不是基于自愿调查，而是基于无处不在的监控。机器学习技术直观地反映了文化的“亲和力”和政治偏好。这些算法根据位置、教育水平、语言、财务状况、财产所有权、职业、年龄、性别、性偏好和关系状态对用户进行排序。他们。

This sinister question—at least, it was meant to sound sinister—headlined the advertising copy for  The 480, a 1964 novel by Eugene Burdick. His previous best sellers,  The Ugly American and  Fail-Safe, had caused sensations in political circles, and the new one promised to do the same. Its jacket featured the image of a punched card. The title referred to 480 categories of voter, defined by region, religion, age, and other demographic characteristics, such as “Midwestern, rural, Protestant, lower income, female.” Many readers recoiled from the notion of being sorted into one of these boxes.  The New York Times’s reviewer called  The 480 a “shock novel” and found it implausible.
 What was so shocking? What was implausible? The idea that a company might use computer technology and behavioral science to gather and crunch data on American citizens, with the nefarious goal of influencing a presidential election.
 In the 1950s and 1960s this seemed like science fiction. Actually,  The 480 was a thinly disguised roman à clef, based on a real-life company called Simulmatics, which had secretly worked for the 1960 campaign of John F. Kennedy. Burdick had been a political operative himself and knew the Simulmatics founders well. The company’s confidential reports and memoranda went straight into his prose. And the 480 categories—listed in an appendix to the novel—were the real Simulmatics voter types, the creation of what one of its founders called “a kind of Manhattan Project gamble in politics.”
 Simulmatics was founded in 1959 and lasted eleven years. Jill Lepore mentioned its involvement in the Kennedy campaign in  These Truths (2018), her monumental history of the United States; she was already on the trail of the story she tells in her new book,  If Then. Lepore is a brilliant and prolific historian with an eye for unusual and revealing stories, and this one is a remarkable saga, sometimes comical, sometimes ominous: a “shadow history of the 1960s,” as she writes, because Simulmatics stumbled through the decade as a bit player, onstage for the Vietnam War, the civil rights movement, the Great Society, the riots and protests. It began with grand ambitions to invent a new kind of predictive behavioral science, in a research environment increasingly tied to a rising defense establishment amid the anxiety of the cold war. It ended ignominiously, in embarrassment and bankruptcy.
 Irving Kristol, the future architect of neoconservativism, dismissed Simulmatics in 1964 as “a struggling little company which, despite the fact that it worked on a few problems for the Kennedy organization in 1960, has since had a difficult time making ends meet,” and he wasn’t wrong. Today it is almost completely forgotten. Yet Lepore finds in it a plausible untold origin story for our current panopticon: a world of constant surveillance, if not by the state then by megacorporations that make vast fortunes by predicting and manipulating our behavior—including, most insidiously, our behavior as voters.
 The name “Simulmatics”—a mashup of “simulation” and “automatic”—was the kind of faux-sophisticated compound that tech startups favored (Teledyne, Biotronik, Microsoft), before a 1990s countertrend led to names like Yahoo, Google, and Twitter. Simulmatics’s creator and president was a former Madison Avenue advertising and public relations executive, Edward L. Greenfield—a glad-handing, scotch-drinking, Pall Mall–smoking huckster, as Lepore describes him. Greenfield saw national politics as virgin territory for a scientifically minded advertising business.
 This was a fairly new idea. In 1952 the eloquent and erudite Democratic nominee, Adlai Stevenson, objected to paying professionals for tools of mass persuasion, while Dwight D. Eisenhower became the first presidential candidate ever to appear in television commercials. A word on people’s lips that year was “brainwashing,” coined by Edward Hunter, the rabidly anti-Communist author of  Brain-Washing in Red China: The Calculated Destruction of Men’s Minds (1951) . Stevenson wanted no part of that. Republicans spent $1.5 million on television advertising, which seems like a pittance until you learn that the Democrats spent $77,000.
 Computers at the time were house-sized machines built from thousands of vacuum tubes, with tanks of liquid mercury serving as “memory.” The largest computer in the world, produced by the Remington Rand corporation for the Census Bureau, was  UNIVAC.  CBS News hired it for election night—Walter Cronkite called it “that miracle of the modern age, the electronic brain.” It was not just going to tally the numbers as they came in; it was meant to predict the outcome before the final results were known. “This is not a joke or a trick,” said his colleague Charles Collingwood. “It’s an experiment, and we think it’s going to work. We don’t know, we hope it will work.” Collingwood referred to the computer as “he”: “He’s sitting there in his corner, humming away. A few minutes ago I asked him what his prediction was, and he sent me back a very caustic answer.” In fact,  UNIVAC’s operators saw early in the evening that Eisenhower seemed to be winning in a landslide, but they weren’t brave enough to trust their computer.
 The pioneers of computing had a dream application for a machine capable of performing rapid calculations on large amounts of data: weather forecasting. Atmospheric behavior is just physics, and the relevant equations were well known. At the Institute for Advanced Study in Princeton, John von Neumann argued that computers would soon enable humans not only to predict the weather but to control it. He believed in a degree of perfection that we now know is unreachable, because the weather is a chaotic system, but the methods he invented are used in the supercomputers that run nonstop at all the world’s meteorological centers. Computers predict the weather by simulating it: by creating models and letting them run.
 Ed Greenfield, the ad man, saw no reason not to try the same approach with the behavior of people. He created a Social Science Division in his company, with the idea of simulating the entire electorate and selling voting predictions to campaigns. He hired a computer guy and a social scientist, Bill McPhee and Ithiel de Sola Pool, and Eugene Burdick, then a political scientist. All of them grew obsessed in one way or another with the possibility of mathematizing human behavior. McPhee was ebullient about programming: “The computers (Univac, Manniac + ‘Johnniac’—I work on Johnniac) are just like big electric trains for grown-up boys.” Pool was working on a theory of “social networks.” He had a notion that “any two Americans, of any sort, can be linked by a chain of six acquaintanceships or less”—six degrees of separation, one might say.
 Who were these people? “They were midcentury white liberals in an era when white liberals were not expected to understand people who weren’t white or liberal,” Lepore says. She calls them—they sometimes called themselves—the What-If Men. Deeply researched, written with elegance and passion,  If Then gives a vivid picture of their lives, including their often miserable wives, suffering “the bad bargains of the middle-class marriages of the 1950s.” In  The 480, Burdick describes the Simulmatics men as a “new underworld”:
 Innocent and well-intentioned people who work with slide rules and calculating machines and computers…. Most of these people are highly educated, many of them are Ph.D.s, and none that I have met have malignant political designs on the American public. They may, however, radically reconstruct the American political system, build a new politics, and even modify revered and venerable American institutions—facts of which they are blissfully innocent.
 Greenfield wanted to build a “Voting Behavior Machine.” He also called it a “People Machine.” It would treat voters as “discrete units at the ‘microscopic’ level,” and it would create a “macroscopic picture of how, when it is all put together, the aggregate system works.” For the first time, large quantities of data had been gathered through the booming business of political polling. When Greenfield and his team formed Simulmatics in advance of the 1960 election, they bought the results of Gallup polls from the past four national elections, 100,000 surveys on punched cards. They sorted the questions into fifty “issue attitudes,” and they sorted the people into demographic boxes.
 First they tried selling their services to Stevenson, their favorite among the potential Democratic candidates. They proposed to test policy positions in the machine before revealing them to actual voters. Should the candidate speak out for civil rights in the South? “We will, from our model, be able to predict what such a speech would mean to each of 1,000 sub-groups of the population…and consequently to pinpoint the state where it could affect the electoral vote,” Greenfield wrote. This was liable to strike some as cynical or sneaky. It should strike us as a description of what every technically astute political consultant offers today.
 When the nomination went instead to Kennedy, they sent their pitch to his campaign. It went like this:
 With speed, accuracy, precision, and efficiency, simulation provides a unique test tube for the political strategist.  Before he acts, it tells him what would be the effect of his handling of certain issues on the voting behavior of the electorate.
  The relationship between such current intelligence and a simulation model developed out of historical data is analogous to the relationship between current weather information and a climatological model.
 The Kennedy team commissioned three reports—urgently, because, for the first time, the rival candidates had agreed to televised debates, and the dates were approaching.
 The Simulmatics men worked fast. No one can say now how much of their advice came from the computer, an  IBM 704 at Columbia University, and how much was old-fashioned political savvy. “There’s a lot of bluster and nonsense in the archival trail left behind by flimflam men,” as Lepore says. But the advice seems to have been good. Black voters were grossly underrepresented in the 480 categories because George Gallup and other pollsters had avoided surveying them—meekly accepting the reality of Jim Crow voter suppression and kowtowing to southern newspaper owners who expressly did not want Blacks polled. But Simulmatics counted them and made the case that Democrats could not win without supporting the civil rights movement. They also advised the candidate to speak out frankly about the anti-Catholicism that dogged his campaign. And they urged him to treat the debates as an opportunity: “The danger to Nixon is that Kennedy can make use of his more personable traits—including a range of emotions such as fervor, humor, friendship, and spirituality.” Kennedy did everything Simulmatics suggested, and when he carried off his narrow victory in November, the What-If Men were sure they deserved the credit.
 And that was it. The sum total of Simulmatics’s intervention in this (or any) political campaign amounted to a few bits of advice, ostensibly computer-generated but really, at most, computer-assisted. No voters were manipulated or deceived. No one’s privacy was invaded.
 The Kennedy team thought they had bought confidential research reports, but the Simulmatics people were desperate for publicity. One of them, Thomas B. Morgan—a friend of Ed Greenfield who had helped him edit the three reports—approached  Harper’s pretending to be a disinterested journalist, and the magazine featured his sensational account in January 1961, the month of Kennedy’s inauguration. “The People-Machine,” screamed the headline. “The First Report on a Computing Device Secretly Designed for the Democratic Presidential Campaign—and on Its Consequences for Political Strategy.” It was a puff piece posing as an exposé. It quoted another Greenfield friend, the Yale political scientist Harold Lasswell, as saying, “This is the A-bomb of the social sciences.”
  A secretly designed robot campaign strategist nicknamed a “people-machine” was said today to have been put to work by President-Elect John F. Kennedy’s top advisers to suggest alternative methods of influencing voters.
 The New York  Herald Tribune said Kennedy’s “secret weapon” had been “a big, bulky monster called a ‘Simulmatics.’” An Oregon paper editorialized, “The voters—you, me, Mrs. Jones next door, and Professor Smith at the university” were reduced “to little holes in punch cards, or whatever device our new Ruler uses.” The Kennedy people panicked and tried to deny the whole thing.
 These still unfamiliar machines— thinking machines—scared people, for good reasons and bad. Machines couldn’t really think, by any definition. They were hardly monsters or robots. They might or might not be threatening to take jobs from people: Kennedy himself had warned on the campaign trail of “the growing crisis of automation—the replacement of men by machines.” Even so, reliance on computers seemed an attack on human agency: you and me, reduced “to little holes in punch cards.” Simulmatics hyped its own power and prowess but also tried to dampen these fears with boilerplate reassurances: “Machines can do nothing but speed up communication.” By doing so, “they restore the possibility of ready discourse about important matters in large societies.” Nowadays, that sounds naive. The same kind of hopeful innocence was heard again three decades later, with the rise of the Internet.
 Computers were, of course, poised to grow in speed and power more rapidly than the most optimistic What-If Man could have dreamed. For Simulmatics, the promise of computing and the hope of predicting human behavior were tied together. “The scientists of the Simulmatics Corporation acted on the proposition that if they could collect enough data about enough people and feed it into a machine,” Lepore writes, “everything, one day, might be predictable, and everyone, every human mind, simulated, each act anticipated, automatically, and even driven and directed, by targeted messages as unerring as missiles.” For Simulmatics, Bill McPhee wrote down what he called “a set of  rules of thinking” to “reproduce or better  simulate the thinking behavior of voters.” (That he wrote the rules from inside the psychiatric ward of Bellevue Hospital, where his wife had temporarily committed him, is the kind of detail a novelist might be ashamed to invent.)
 Around the same time, the science-fiction writer Isaac Asimov began his  Foundation series of novels, premised on the idea of a science of psychohistory. The sages who mastered this science could predict the rise and fall of empires: “Psychohistory was the quintessence of sociology; it was the science of human behavior reduced to mathematical equations.”  1 Asimov’s fictional future didn’t need computers to predict its future—just geniuses, making magic with their formulas. The difference between Asimov and Simulmatics was that Asimov knew he was spinning a fantasy. People do not behave as predictably as billiard balls or water molecules. Atmospheric simulation relies on variables like temperature, pressure, and humidity; the science of psychology has discovered no mental or social quantities that can be defined as simply or measured as precisely. No matter how powerful our computers are, we have no scientific laws of behavior to feed them.
 Having begun the 1960s with a storm of publicity, Simulmatics set out to expand its reach from campaign politics to consumer marketing. The company pitched projects to film studios and music labels, along with advertising agencies—anyone they thought might buy the notion of simulating the needs and wants of masses of consumers. It offered, Lepore writes, “a simulated population, a miniature United States, consisting of three thousand perfectly representative but entirely imaginary people.”  Punch, the British satirical magazine, mocked this new approach to reading society’s mind: “ Punch proposed that Simulmatics’ Media-Mix add a few more categories,” Lepore writes,
 including “dog-lover, flat-earther, doughnut dunker, milk-in-firster,” so as to be able to determine, for instance, whether a new, seventy-five-cent pink ballpoint pen ink refill would be desired by consumers who shop on Wednesdays, have Republican sympathies, “fruit-juice breakfasts, bouts of depression, slight astigmatism, fitted carpets, thick eyebrows, and one or more cousins in the armed forces.”
  The company also pursued government contracts. Its social scientist, Pool, now chairing MIT’s new political science department, got the Defense Department’s Advanced Research Project Agency to finance a foray into psychological warfare to be called Project ComCom, for Communist Communications. As the United States committed itself more and more deeply to the war in Vietnam, Simulmatics went there, too. After all, psychological warfare was just political persuasion militarized, and here was fertile new territory. “Vietnam is the greatest social-science laboratory we have ever had!” Pool told a colleague. Robert McNamara, the numerically oriented secretary of defense, wanted to win hearts and minds, and along with the soldiers he brought in behavioral scientists as civilian contractors.
 Simulmatics and its much larger competitor, the  RAND Corporation, set up shop in nearby Saigon villas. One of  RAND’s analysts was a former marine named Daniel Ellsberg, who later helped write and then leaked the devastating secret report known as the Pentagon Papers. These researchers were an odd presence in a bloody war. “Young men from  RAND and Simulmatics bounded about the countryside in Land Rovers studying ‘upward mobility among village elites’ or ‘the interrelationship of land reform with peasant political motivation,’” wrote Frances FitzGerald in her prize-winning history  Fire in the Lake. Twenty-six years old when she arrived in Vietnam, she thought they were remarkably naive.
 By Lepore’s account, the Simulmatics operation was something of a clown show. Its staffers, who spoke little or no Vietnamese, got their fingers into various pieces of the US adventure, including the notorious pacification program as well as a psychological warfare project known as Chieu Hoi. These relied more on old-fashioned propaganda methods—and brutal coercion—than anything involving special data analysis. Ed Greenfield arrived in 1966 with a plan to set up a “paramilitary action research center.” But the Pentagon was realizing how much the company had overpromised and how little it had done, and at the end of 1967 the team was sent home.
 On their way to obscurity, the Simulmatics people played minor parts in major events, appearing Zelig-like at crucial moments of 1960s history. After race riots in Detroit, Los Angeles, and other cities, Simulmatics conducted a study for the Kerner Commission, established by Lyndon Johnson to investigate the riots and their causes. One sociologist told the commission, “Until we can predict riots with at least as much reliability as the weather forecast predicts rain or snow, it will be very difficult rationally to devise policies of control.” That was a last gasp. In 1968 the company shut down for good, bankrupt. It stiffed its last consultants, including future senator Daniel Patrick Moynihan. It had accomplished nothing.
 What seemed frightening and even immoral fifty years ago we now mostly take for granted. We shouldn’t. Facebook places its billions of customers into many more than 480 categories, based not on voluntary surveys but on pervasive surveillance. Machine-learning techniques intuit cultural “affinities” and political preferences. The algorithms sort users by location, education level, languages, financial standing, property ownership, occupation, age, gender, sexual preference, and relationship status. They 
......

该存储库包含一个教程，旨在向您介绍在Linux内核中为快速数据路径(XDP)系统有效编写程序所需的基本步骤，该系统提供与内核集成的高性能可编程数据包处理。
本教程由许多课程组成，每个课程都有自己的存储库。从课程“basicXX”开始，阅读每个存储库中的Readme.org文件以获取该课程的说明。
请继续阅读下面的内容，了解XDP的介绍和在本教程中将学到的概述，或者直接跳到第一课。
XDP是上游Linux内核的一部分，使用户能够在内核对数据进行任何其他处理之前，将对每个到达的数据包执行的数据包处理程序安装到内核中。这些程序用受限的C编写，并编译成eBPF字节码格式，在经过安全性验证后在内核中执行和JIT编译。这种方法提供了极大的灵活性和高性能，并与系统的其余部分很好地集成在一起。有关XDP的一般介绍，请阅读学术论文(Pdf)或cilium BPF参考指南。
本教程旨在实际介绍使用XDP系统成功编写有用程序所需的不同步骤。我们假设您对Linux网络以及如何使用iproute2工具套件进行配置有基本的了解，但假定您以前没有使用eBPF或XDP的经验。
该教程正在进行中，将于2019年3月在布拉格举行的Netdev会议上试运行。然而，我们希望它(最终)是自给自足的，任何人都可以通过它来学习XDP基础知识。非常欢迎为实现这一目标提供意见和贡献；只需在Github存储库中公开问题或拉出请求即可。
在开始完成本教程中的步骤之前，您需要在系统上安装一些依赖项。Setup_Dependencies.org中对这些内容进行了说明。
我们还提供了一个帮助器脚本，它将设置一个带有虚拟接口的测试环境，以便您在其上测试代码。这是在基础课程中介绍的，也有它自己的自述文件。
本教程分为多个课程；每个课程都有自己的子目录，并且课程按类别分组：
我们建议您从“基本”课程开始，然后按数字顺序学习每个类别中的课程。有关如何完成课程的说明，请阅读每个课程目录中的Readme.org文件。
我们建议您从这些课程开始，因为它们将教您如何编译和检查将实现数据包处理代码的eBPF程序，如何将它们加载到内核中，以及如何在之后检查状态。作为基本课程的一部分，您还将编写一个eBPF程序加载器，您将在后续课程中使用该程序加载器。
一旦您弄清楚了基础知识并知道如何将程序加载到内核中，您就可以开始处理一些数据包了。数据包进度类别中的课程将教您处理数据包所需的不同步骤，包括解析、重写、指示内核在处理后如何处理数据包，以及如何使用帮助器访问现有的内核功能。
完成基本和数据包处理类别的课程后，您应该已经准备好编写第一个真正的XDP程序，该程序将对进入系统的数据包进行有用的处理。但是，还有一些稍微高级的主题，一旦您开始扩展程序以做更多事情，这些主题可能会很有用。
高级课程中涵盖的主题包括如何使内核其他部分中的eBPF程序与您的XDP程序交互、在程序之间传递元数据、与用户空间和内核功能交互的最佳实践，以及如何在单个界面上运行多个XDP程序。
note_en:
This repository contains a tutorial that aims to introduce you to the basic steps needed to effectively write programs for the eXpress Data Path (XDP) system in the Linux kernel, which offers high-performance programmable packet processing integrated with the kernel.
 The tutorial is composed of a number of lessons, each of which has its own repository. Start with the lessons starting with “basicXX”, and read the README.org file in each repository for instructions for that lesson.
 Keep reading below for an introduction to XDP and an overview of what you will learn in this tutorial, or jump  straight to the first lesson.
    XDP is a part of the upstream Linux kernel, and enables users to install packet processing programs into the kernel, that will be executed for each arriving packet, before the kernel does any other processing on the data. The programs are written in restricted C, and compiled into the eBPF byte code format that is executed and JIT-compiled in the kernel, after being verified for safety. This approach offers great flexibility and high performance, and integrates well with the rest of the system. For a general introduction to XDP, read  the academic paper (pdf), or the  Cilium BPF reference guide.
 This tutorial aims to be a practical introduction to the different steps needed to successfully write useful programs using the XDP system. We assume you have a basic understanding of Linux networking and how to configure it with the  iproute2 suite of tools, but assume no prior experience with eBPF or XDP.
 The tutorial is a work in progress, and will be trial-run at the  Netdev Conference in Prague in March 2019. However, we intend for it to (eventually) be self-contained and something that anyone can go through to learn the XDP basics. Input and contributions to advance towards this goal are very welcome; just open issues or pull requests in the  Github repository.
  Before you can start completing step in this tutorial, you will need to install a few dependencies on your system. These are described in  setup_dependencies.org.
 We also provide a helper script that will set up a test environment with virtual interfaces for you to test your code on. This is introduced in the basic lessons, and also has  it’s own README file.
  The tutorial is organised into a number of lessons; each lesson has its own subdirectory, and the lessons are grouped by category:
  We recommend you start with the “basic” lessons, and follow the lessons in each category in numerical order. Read the README.org file in each lesson directory for instructions on how to complete the lesson.
  We recommend you start with these lessons, as they will teach you how to compile and inspect the eBPF programs that will implement your packet processing code, how to load them into the kernel, and how to inspect the state afterwards. As part of the basic lessons you will also be writing an eBPF program loader that you will need in subsequent lessons.
  Once you have the basics figured out and know how to load programs into the kernel, you are ready to start processing some packets. The lessons in the packet progressing category will teach you about the different steps needed to process data packets, including parsing, rewriting, instructing the kernel about what to do with the packet after processing, and how to use helpers to access existing kernel functionality.
  After having completed the lessons in the basic and packet processing categories, you should be all set to write your first real XDP program that will do useful processing of the packets coming into the system. However, there are some slightly more advanced topics that will probably be useful once you start expanding your program to do more things.
 The topics covered in the advanced lessons include how to make eBPF programs in other parts of the kernel interact with your XDP program, passing metadata between programs, best practices for interacting with userspace and kernel features, and how to run multiple XDP programs on a single interface.
title: XDP实践教程

note:
2013年9月17日，一款游戏发布，彻底改变了流行文化。侠盗猎车手V的成功程度是无法用语言形容的…。但是你点击了这个页面，所以我会尽力的。
这个七周年纪念日有一些偶然的事情。2013年GTA V发布时，距离Xbox One和PS4发布只有2个月的时间。现在，2020年，我们距离Xbox Series X和PlayStation5还有两个月的时间。有趣的是，侠盗猎车手V也将推出这两款游戏机。这是一款广受欢迎的游戏，它在三代游戏机上毫不费力地存活了下来。
但我想分析一下Rockstar Games是如何保持这种长寿的。所以，让我们回到2013年的那一天。我清楚地记得比赛第一个小时的黑夜和白昼的对比。你从寒冷、沉闷的中西部虚构的北扬克顿州开始。一起银行抢劫案向南行进，导致你向西前往蓝天灿烂的圣安德烈亚斯州。
你开着一辆华丽的跑车在洛斯桑托斯阳光普照的街道上穿梭在车流和停车场之间。这个世界有着令人难以置信的丰富多彩，让我立马就被吸引住了。电台的特色是所有这些令人难以置信的歌曲和艺术家，以及讽刺而又令人着迷的一系列主持人和商业广告。摩天大楼让人感觉很大。行人感觉很特别。从GTA 4到GTA 5有了令人难以置信的飞跃。
摇滚明星用一个丰富的故事吸引了我们所有人，这个故事呼应了电影中的犯罪经典，比如热火。定位球很壮观，而且层次分明。人物很丰富，并且考虑到了这些相互交织的故事。侠盗猎车手V带来了前所未有的范围和规模。
是的，我们还有很多其他的开放世界的游戏。是的，我们看到了电影故事的兴起。但侠盗猎车手V的操作水平是所有其他游戏都希望它能操作的。即使到今天，许多游戏与2013年的冠军相比也相形见绌。有人可能希望开发人员追逐GTAV中的想法和概念，但没有多少人这样做。
没有任何其他有三个主角的主要游戏可以在飞行中切换。与侠盗猎车手3的潮流追逐者不同，似乎每个人都意识到他们无法与GTA V的雄心和规模相提并论。到处都是快艇、飞机、轰鸣的汽车和混乱的大操场是无与伦比的。
有很多游戏可以创造巨大的繁荣和汽车追逐，但并不是所有的游戏都做得很好。声音，图形，枪战，车辆的操控，它们都在这里融合在一起，创造出美妙的东西。当然，所有这些都为让GTA V统治了7年的GTA Online奠定了基础。
Rockstar让玩家在发布后等待两周才能玩到雄心勃勃的新多人模式。这让人们有足够的时间专注于竞选活动，并在2013年10月网络下线时全力以赴。这个想法很大，也是粉丝们梦寐以求的。在这个世界里，你爬上犯罪黑社会的行列，积累财富和资产。
你和其他十几个人会在一个大厅里，在那里你可以在这个讽刺天使的城市里战斗或联合起来。你的衣服、车辆和家都是身份的象征。当然，就像现实生活一样，人们非常想要这些身份象征。他们找到了利用这个游戏的方法，给他们带来了深不可测的财富。
这在游戏中的经济中创造了这种令人着迷的失衡，而游戏仍然受到这种失衡的困扰。虽然2013年游戏中最好的车大约是100万美元，但现在有很多车的价值是这个数字的两倍甚至更多。Rockstar已经尽了最大努力来对付这些作弊者，但当你的游戏销量超过1.35亿份时，就很难控制了。
许多人想知道为什么这款游戏还在卖拷贝。你可能认为现在每个人都有一个副本，但侠盗猎车手的美妙之处在于它是这么多年轻游戏玩家的白鲸。这是叛逆的游戏，是电子游戏的摇滚乐。你的父母告诉你，你长大后不能玩它，只会让你更想要它。
当他们最终长大到可以玩的时候，他们就会出去买。每年都会有新的玩家前来，因为这就是传说中的宝石。在侠盗猎车手VI上映之前，这个循环可能永远不会结束。
当YouTube的游戏场景真正开始达到顶峰时，侠盗猎车手V也上映了。像公鸡牙齿这样的频道以有趣和有创意的方式促进了游戏的发展，为没有游戏的人提供了一个进入游戏的门户。其他渠道引发了游戏中的阴谋，比如围绕游戏最高山峰的谜团。一些人认为里面有一个喷气背包。也许有外星人参与其中。有些人甚至发现了鬼魂。
Rockstar创造了一款适合社交媒体的游戏，因为它正开始真正成为营销游戏的巨大工具。归功于YouTubers，侠盗猎车手V本质上是在推销自己。截至2019年，这款游戏在所有关于这款游戏的视频中已经获得了近400亿的点击量。这是当年YouTube上观看人数第三多的视频游戏。
同样的支持也让这款游戏获得了频繁的更新。我们有抢劫案，有房子，有工作中的赌场，还有更多。这个游戏的范围和规模只会变得越来越疯狂。当它开始的时候，它只是一个简单的白手起家的在线游戏。如果你愿意，它可能会变得古怪，但它仍然是合情合理的。
现在，在2020年，有激光枪、不明飞行物、会飞的汽车和自行车，还有更多。还记得第一部“速度与激情”是关于偷DVD播放机的吗？现在，很明显，第九部电影要去太空了。这就是侠盗猎车手V的样子，除了在一场比赛中。
随着我们接近2021年，侠盗猎车手V将继续扩张。GTA Online的新地点计划在2020年，下一代版本将于2021年发布，独家更新。除了GTA VI之外，可能永远不会有任何东西像GTA V这样大，而且得到了很好的支持。
虽然我们不知道侠盗猎车手6什么时候上映，但有一件事可能是肯定的。当它发布时，Rockstar可能会有足够的资金开始制造真正的飞行汽车。
note_en:
On September 17th, 2013, a game released that changed pop-culture forever.  Grand Theft Auto V had a level of success that can’t really be put into words… but you clicked on this page, so I’ll try my best.
 There’s something serendipitous about this 7 year anniversary. When GTA V released in 2013, it was just 2 months before the release of the Xbox One and PS4. Now, in 2020, we’re 2 months away from  Xbox Series X and  PlayStation 5. Funnily enough, Grand Theft Auto V is coming to those consoles as well. It’s a game that is so wildly popular it has survived three console generations without taking a moment to breathe.
 But I want to analyze how Rockstar Games has maintained that longevity. So, let’s go back to that day in 2013. I vividly remember the night and day contrast of the game’s first hour. You start out in the cold, dreary fictional Midwest state of North Yankton. A bank heist goes south, resulting in you heading west to the blue-skied state of San Andreas.
  You hit the sun-soaked streets of Los Santos in a flashy sports car, weaving through traffic and parking garages. The world had this unbelievable richness that captured me with such immediacy. The radio featured all these incredible songs and artists as well as a satirical yet enrapturing series of hosts and commercials. The skyscrapers felt large. The pedestrians felt unique. There was an unbelievable leap from GTA 4 to GTA 5.
 Rockstar sucked us all in with a rich story that echoed cinematic crime classics like Heat. The set pieces were spectacular and layered. The characters were rich and allowed for these intertwining stories. Grand Theft Auto V brought a scope and scale never seen before by any other game.
 Yes, we had numerous other open-world games. Yes, we were seeing the rise of cinematic stories. But Grand Theft Auto V operated at a level that every other game wished it could operate at. Even to this day, many games pale in comparison to the 2013 title. One might have expected developers to chase the ideas and concepts from GTA V but not many did.
  There aren’t any other major games with three protagonists you can switch between on the fly. Unlike the trendchasers of Grand Theft Auto 3, it seems everyone recognized they weren’t going to match GTA V’s ambition and scale. The big playground filled with speed boats, planes, roaring cars, and chaos is unrivaled.
 There are a lot of games that can create big booms and car chases but not all of them do it well. The sound, the graphics, the gunplay, the handling of the vehicles, it all melds together here to create something wonderful. Of course, all of this laid the foundation for what has allowed GTA V to dominate for 7 years: GTA Online.
 Rockstar made players wait 2 weeks after launch to play the ambitious new multiplayer mode. This gave people plenty of time to focus on campaign and go all-in on the online when it dropped in October 2013. The idea was big and something fans had long dreamed of. A world where you climbed the ranks of the criminal underworld, amassing riches and assets.
  You and over a dozen other people would be in a lobby where you could fight or join forces in the satirical city of angels. Your clothes, vehicles, and home were a sign of status. Of course, like real life, people wanted those status symbols badly. They found ways to exploit the game, giving them unfathomable levels of wealth.
 This created this fascinating in-balance in the in-game economy which the game still suffers from. While the best car in the game in 2013 was about a million dollars, there are many cars now worth double that or more. Rockstar has done their best to counter these cheaters but when you have a game that has sold over  135 million copies, it becomes hard to control.
 Many people wonder how the game is still selling copies. You’d think everyone has a copy by now but the beauty of Grand Theft Auto is that its the white whale of so many younger gamers. It’s the rebellious game, it’s the rock and roll of video games. It’s the one your parents tell you that you can’t play growing up, making you only want it more.
  When they eventually become old enough to play it, they go out and buy it. A new audience of players comes in year after year because it’s this fabled gem. It’s this cycle that will likely never end until Grand Theft Auto VI releases.
 Grand Theft Auto V also released when YouTube’s gaming scene was really starting to peak. Channels like Rooster Teeth boosted the game with fun and creative let’s plays, giving a portal into the game for people who didn’t have it. Other channels stoked the fires of in-game conspiracies such as a mystery surrounding the game’s tallest mountain. Some believed a jetpack lied within it. Maybe aliens were involved. Some people even found ghosts.
 Rockstar created a game ripe for social media right as it was starting to really become a huge tool used for marketing games. Grand Theft Auto V essentially marketed itself thanks to YouTubers. As of 2019, the game had gained nearly 40 billion views across all the videos about the game. It was the third highest viewed video game of that year on YouTube.
  That same support has also allowed the game to get frequent updates. We’ve gotten  heists, houses,  a working casino, and more. The scope and scale of the game has only gotten more and more insane. When it began, it was a simple rags to riches online game. It could get whacky if you wanted but it was still grounded within reason.
 Now, in 2020, there’s laser guns,  UFOs, flying cars and bikes, and much more. Remember how the first Fast and Furious was about stealing DVD players? Now the 9th movie is going to space, apparently. That’s what Grand Theft Auto V is like, except all in one game.
 As we approach 2021, Grand Theft Auto V will continue to expand.  A new location for GTA Online is planned for 2020 and a next-gen version will release in 2021 with exclusive updates. There will probably never be anything quite as big and well-supported as GTA V besides GTA VI.
 While we have no idea when  Grand Theft Auto VI is coming, one thing is probably certain. When it releases, Rockstar will probably have enough money to start manufacturing real flying cars.
title: 侠盗飞车V今天7岁了
title_en: Grand Theft Auto V is 7 years old today
